summary: The author argues that warnings about Moore's Law ending have become a tired refrain that no longer carries weight after decades of repetition. She points out that developers who care about performance are already writing code that handles the supposed threats—parallelization, distributed systems, and leveraging new hardware. The post contends that modern performance concerns are far more nuanced than raw processor speed, encompassing network latency, I/O bottlenecks, and data volume. Rather than fearing some apocalyptic end to Moore's Law, practical engineers have been continuously adapting their approaches as technology evolves.
key_points: Moore's Law warnings have been repeated for over a decade without the predicted catastrophic impact materializing
Developers who care about performance are already writing parallel and distributed code—they don't wait for crisis
The 'circle of tech life' means continuously adopting new approaches (parallelism, distributed systems, SSDs) as they become available
Modern performance concerns are multifaceted: network sensitivity, I/O bounds, data volume—not just processor speed
The bogeyman of Moore's Law ending is unnecessary fear-mongering for people already doing performance-conscious work
money_quotes: For as long as I can remember, Moore's Law has been one of the great tech bogeymen.
When you hear the same message for well over ten years, its efficacy starts to fade a bit.
The truth of the matter is this: if you care about Moore's Law, you're probably already writing code that combats it, because you care about performance.
The circle of tech life takes advantage of the latest hotness as needed to get the job done.
These days most of us are concerned about a much more complex interaction of performance issues than simple processor speed.
He's sitting in the next cube, making sure we parallelized all our outgoing requests, and he'd rather you stopped getting so hysterical on his behalf.
key_insight: Threatening developers with Moore's Law ending is pointless because those who care about performance are already adapting continuously, and modern performance challenges extend far beyond processor speed.
